questions.text: Conversion of ammonium into nitrates is:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Nitrosomonas convert ammonia into nitrates by a process called nitrification. Nitrogen fixation is a process by which molecular nitrogen in the air is converted into ammonia. Ammonification refers to any chemical reaction in which NH2 groups are converted into ammonia or its ionic form, ammonium (NH4+), as an end product.Denitrification is the microbial process of reducing nitrate and nitrite to gaseous forms of nitrogen, principally nitrous oxide (N2O) and nitrogen (N2)

questions.text: In a forest, there are a lot of plants; trees, shrubs, and herbs. What will the Palm trees face if they grow in the same forest?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: In a forest, palm trees have to face all sorts of competition (intraspecific, interspecific, and environmental). Intraspecific competition is a competition of a species among its own type, palm trees will have to face competition from other palm trees already in that forest. Interspecific competition is competition among different species, palm trees will have to face competition from other species. Environmental competition is the competition exerted by the environment (availability of light, water, and nutrients) on species. Hence option D is correct.

questions.text: The animals that feed on organic debris from decomposing plants and animals are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The plant-eater animal is called herbivores. e.g; reptiles, birds, and mammals.
The meat eater or animal eaters are called carnivores.
The animals which eat both plants and animals' food are called omnivores. e.g; bear, man, pigs, etc.
Detritivores are animals that feed on detritus or organic debris. Detritus is an organic debris obtained from decomposing plants and animals e.g; Earthworm.

questions.explanation: Detritivores, also known as detritophages, detritus feeders, detritus eaters, or saprophages, are heterotrophs that obtain nutrients by consuming detritus (decomposing plant and animal parts as well as feces). By doing so, all these detritivores contribute to decomposition and the nutrient cycles.

questions.text: Process ensuring the survival of species over long periods of time, even though individual members of the species die.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Reproduction is the biological process by which new individual organisms "offspring" are produced from their "parents" Adaptability means being able to rapidly learn new skills and behaviours in response to changing circumstances. Mitosis is a process where a single cell divides into two identical daughter cells. Respiration is a chemical reaction that happens in all living cells, including plant cells and animal cells. It is the way that energy is released from glucose so that all the other chemical processes needed for life can happen.

questions.text: The position of an organism in a food chain of an ecosystem called:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Option A most likely refers to levels of ecology (since there isn’t such a thing as level of ecosystem), which are organism, population, community, ecosystem, biosphere. Option B refers to a food chain which describes the linear transfer of energy from producers to consumers. Option C describes the interconnection of multiple food chains within a certain community. Option D is the correct answer, as it describes the position or the level of a certain organism in a food web or a food chain. Option E is a graphical representation of a food chain, which describes the energy levels and energy transfer from one trophic level to the next.

questions.text: The productivity of aquatic ecosystem is determined by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water is a part of the environment for aquatic organisms as such it becomes a constant and it has no effect on the productivity of aquatic ecosystems.
Aquatic organisms require both light and nutrients for their growth and development so both have an equal effect on the productivity of aquatic ecosystems.
Light is a requirement of aquatic ecosystems so variation in the amount of light causes variation in the productivity of ecosystems.
Nutrients are a requirement of aquatic ecosystems so variation in the amount of nutrients causes variation in the productivity of aquatic ecosystems.

questions.explanation: The productivity of the aquatic ecosystem is determined on the basis of availability of light and nutrients to it, hence, option B is correct. Water is present in all aquatic ecosystems regardless.

questions.text: The diagram shows a simplified nitrogen cycle. Which row shows the correct labels for P, Q, R, and S?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The conversion of nitrates in the soil to nitrogen is called the denitrifying(P) process, which is carried out by denitrifying bacteria. Then this nitrogen is fixed(Q) into the legume plants, which can form ammonia either by ammonification by saprotrophic fungi(S) or decay of leaf tissue(R). Hence the answer is A. ( p ) Denitrification. Denitrification is the process that converts nitrate to nitrogen gas, thus removing bioavailable nitrogen and returning it to the atmosphere. Dinitrogen gas (N2) is the ultimate end product of denitrification, but other intermediate gaseous forms of nitrogen exist. ( q ) Legumes can form a symbiotic relationship with nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria called rhizobia. The result of this symbiosis is to form nodules on the plant root, within which the bacteria can convert atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia that can be used by the plant. ( r ) The process of ammonification is carried out by certain bacteria just as Bacillus ramosus, B. vulgaris, Clostridium, Actinomyces, etc. In this process, these organisms release a specific amount of nitrogen compounds like ammonia. However, saprophytic bacteria will not be involved in this process. ( s ) Plant and animal wastes decompose, adding nitrogen to the soil. Bacteria in the soil convert those forms of nitrogen into forms plants can use. Plants use the nitrogen in the soil to grow. People and animals eat the plants; then animal and plant residues return nitrogen to the soil again, completing the cycle.
This row incorrectly labels Q as "Nitrogen fixation by nitrifying bacteria." Nitrifying bacteria actually convert ammonia (NH3) into nitrites (NO2-) and nitrates (NO3-), not the other way around.
This row incorrectly labels both P and Q. Denitrification is not performed by anaerobic bacteria, and nitrogen fixation is not performed using the nitrogenase enzyme.
This row incorrectly labels P, Q, and S. Denitrification does not produce nitrate, nitrogen fixation does not occur in the roots of legumes, and assimilation does not involve urea.

questions.explanation: The conversion of nitrates in the soil to nitrogen is called the denitrifying(P) process, which is carried out by denitrifying bacteria. Then this nitrogen is fixed(Q) into the legume plants, which can form ammonia either by ammonification by saprotrophic fungi(S) or decay of leaf tissue(R). Hence the answer is A. ( p ) Denitrification. Denitrification is the process that converts nitrate to nitrogen gas, thus removing bioavailable nitrogen and returning it to the atmosphere. Dinitrogen gas (N2) is the ultimate end product of denitrification, but other intermediate gaseous forms of nitrogen exist. ( q ) Legumes can form a symbiotic relationship with nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria called rhizobia. The result of this symbiosis is to form nodules on the plant root, within which the bacteria can convert atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia that can be used by the plant. ( r ) The process of ammonification is carried out by certain bacteria just as Bacillus ramosus, B. vulgaris, Clostridium, Actinomyces, etc. In this process, these organisms release a specific amount of nitrogen compounds like ammonia. However, saprophytic bacteria will not be involved in this process. ( s ) Plant and animal wastes decompose, adding nitrogen to the soil. Bacteria in the soil convert those forms of nitrogen into forms plants can use. Plants use the nitrogen in the soil to grow. People and animals eat the plants; then animal and plant residues return nitrogen to the soil again, completing the cycle.

questions.text: The diagram shows a food web. Which organisms occupy the same trophic level?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect according to the given diagram.
Incorrect according to the given diagram.
Incorrect according to the given diagram.
Incorrect according to the given diagram.
Trophic level is determined by the place the organism occupies after the producer trophic level. Thus, 1 and 2 are both at the same trophic level. Being just at the next position after producer.

questions.explanation: Trophic level is determined by the place the organism occupies after the producer trophic level. Thus, 1 and 2 are both at the same trophic level. Being just at the next position after producer. All other options are incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true about Savannah?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Dry season is very long and the temperature ranges more than 18 C throughout the year
options.explanation: While savannas do have a prolonged dry season, the temperature variation throughout the year is generally less extreme than 18°C (32°F). The average temperature in savannas typically ranges from 20 to 30°C (68 to 86°F) in the wet season and 10 to 20°C (50 to 68°F) in the dry season.

options.text: Its plants do not shed off their leaves
options.explanation: Some plants found in savannas are evergreen, meaning they retain their leaves throughout the year. However, many savanna plants, particularly grasses, are deciduous, meaning they shed their leaves during the dry season to conserve water.

Savannas are typically found in tropical and subtropical regions, where the subsoil does not freeze permanently. The soil in savannas may experience some drying during the dry season, but it does not remain frozen year-round.

options.text: Rainfall is up to 200 cm per year
options.explanation: Savannas are characterized by a distinct wet and dry season. The wet season typically brings heavy rainfall, with annual precipitation ranging from 50 to 200 centimeters (20 to 80 inches). This abundant rainfall supports the growth of tall grasses and scattered trees, which are the defining features of savannas.

Savannas typically have a higher rate of evaporation than rainfall, leading to a water deficit during the dry season. This water deficit is a major stressor for savanna plants and animals, and it plays a role in shaping the unique ecology of these ecosystems.

questions.explanation: The Savanna biome is part of a larger grassland biome and is mainly made up of flat grassland vegetation. There are two distinct seasons consisting of a wet and a dry season. The Savanna biome has an average temperature of 25oC. It goes as high as 30oC during the summer and as low as 20oC during the winter, annually.

questions.text: Four words are shown below: Facultative obligate saprophytes parasites These words can be used in the basis of P Q R and S to complete the sentence below. Organisms that depend on living plants and animals for the nutritional requirements are known as…. P… Parasites, which depend on the nutrition entirely on other living organisms, are known as...Q... or total parasites and those which depend for these requirements partially on other living organisms are called...R...or partial parasite on the other hand the plants which depend on dead or rotten organic remains of plants and animals are called...S…
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect answer.

Parasite is an organism that lives in or on an organism of another specie (its host) and benefits by deriving nutrients at the other's expense. An obligate parasite or holoparasite is a parasitic organism that can not complete its life-cycle without exploiting a suitable host. If an obligate parasite can not obtain a host, it will fail to reproduce. Saprotrophic nutrition or lysotrophic nutrition is a process of chemoheterotrophic extracellular digestion involved in the processing of decayed organic matter. It occurs in saprotrophs and is most often associated with fungi and soil bacteria. A facultative parasite is an organism that may resort to parasitic activity but does not absolutely rely on any host for completion of its life cycle. Examples of facultative parasitism occur among many species of fungi, such as family members of the genus Armillaria. So, Option b is the correct answer.
Incorrect answer.
Incorrect answer.

questions.explanation: Parasite is an organism that lives in or on an organism of another specie (its host) and benefits by deriving nutrients at the other's expense. An obligate parasite or holoparasite is a parasitic organism that can not complete its life-cycle without exploiting a suitable host. If an obligate parasite can not obtain a host, it will fail to reproduce. Saprotrophic nutrition or lysotrophic nutrition is a process of chemoheterotrophic extracellular digestion involved in the processing of decayed organic matter. It occurs in saprotrophs and is most often associated with fungi and soil bacteria. A facultative parasite is an organism that may resort to parasitic activity but does not absolutely rely on any host for completion of its life cycle. Examples of facultative parasitism occur among many species of fungi, such as family members of the genus Armillaria. So, Option b is the correct answer.

questions.text: Flow of energy in an ecosystem is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option means that the flow of energy in an ecosystem is one-way, meaning it moves from one trophic level to the next in a linear fashion. Energy enters the ecosystem through the primary producers (plants or photosynthetic organisms), is passed on to the primary consumers (herbivores), then to secondary consumers (carnivores or omnivores), and so on. Energy flow is unidirectional because energy is not recycled back to previous trophic levels.

This term is not commonly used in ecological contexts and doesn't accurately describe the flow of energy in an ecosystem. It implies that energy flows in three different directions, which is not the case.

Similar to "tridirectional," this term is not used to describe the flow of energy in an ecosystem. It suggests that energy flows in multiple directions simultaneously, which is not an accurate representation of energy flow.

This option implies that energy flows in two directions within an ecosystem. While there may be some instances where energy can move in more than one direction (e.g., through decomposition and detritus food chains), the overall flow of energy in an ecosystem is primarily unidirectional, as explained above.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "Unidirectional."Explanation of options:Unidirectional: This option means that the flow of energy in an ecosystem is one-way, meaning it moves from one trophic level to the next in a linear fashion. Energy enters the ecosystem through the primary producers (plants or photosynthetic organisms), is passed on to the primary consumers (herbivores), then to secondary consumers (carnivores or omnivores), and so on. Energy flow is unidirectional because energy is not recycled back to previous trophic levels.Tridirectional: This term is not commonly used in ecological contexts and doesn't accurately describe the flow of energy in an ecosystem. It implies that energy flows in three different directions, which is not the case.Multidirectional: Similar to "tridirectional," this term is not used to describe the flow of energy in an ecosystem. It suggests that energy flows in multiple directions simultaneously, which is not an accurate representation of energy flow.Bidirectional: This option implies that energy flows in two directions within an ecosystem. While there may be some instances where energy can move in more than one direction (e.g., through decomposition and detritus food chains), the overall flow of energy in an ecosystem is primarily unidirectional, as explained above.In summary, the flow of energy in an ecosystem is primarily unidirectional, moving from lower trophic levels to higher trophic levels.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Overgrazing refers to the excessive grazing of an area by livestock, which leads to the depletion of vegetation and soil erosion. When the vegetation is gone and the soil is eroded, the land can become barren and unsuitable for grazing or other uses. Therefore Option A is correct.

Overgrazing does not result in good pasture lands. Quite the opposite, overgrazing can lead to the deterioration of pasture lands. When vegetation is excessively grazed without adequate time for regeneration, the land may become overused and unable to support healthy growth. Therefore, Option B is not correct.

Overgrazing can contribute to salinity issues in certain contexts. When vegetation is overgrazed, it can result in increased soil erosion and decreased vegetation cover. This can lead to increased soil exposure to elements like wind and water, which can cause the accumulation of salts on the surface and potentially result in salinity problems. Hence Salinity is also a possible consequence of overgrazing in certain circumstances, but it is not the most accurate answer.

Overgrazing does not directly result in the formation of rocky areas. However, it can indirectly contribute to erosion and soil degradation, which may expose underlying rocks and contribute to the formation of rocky areas over time. Therefore, Option D is not the direct consequence of overgrazing.

questions.explanation: Overgrazing refers to the excessive grazing of an area by livestock, which leads to the depletion of vegetation and soil erosion. When the vegetation is gone, and the soil is eroded, the land can become totally barren and unsuitable for grazing or other uses.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correct for food web?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A food web is a graphical representation of interconnected food chains. It shows the feeding relationships and energy flow between different organisms in an ecosystem. A food web starts with primary producers, such as plants, which are then consumed by primary consumers, such as herbivores. The primary consumers are then consumed by secondary consumers, such as carnivores, and so on. Therefore, the statement "Start with primary consumers" is not correct for a food web.

A food web is formed by interconnected food chains. It represents the complex network of feeding relationships in an ecosystem, where multiple food chains intersect and interact. Unlike a linear food chain that shows a single pathway of energy transfer, a food web accounts for the interconnectedness and multiple feeding relationships among different organisms.

A food web is generally considered more stable than a food chain. This is because a food web incorporates multiple interconnected pathways for energy transfer and nutrient cycling. If one specie or link in the food web is disrupted, there are alternative pathways and connections that can compensate for the loss. This redundancy and complexity enhance the stability of the overall system, making it less vulnerable to disturbances compared to a linear food chain.

A food web is more complex than a food chain. While a food chain represents a single linear pathway of energy transfer from one organism to another, a food web encompasses multiple interconnected food chains, forming a complex network of feeding relationships. It reflects the intricate interactions and interdependencies among various organisms in an ecosystem, including multiple trophic levels and the flow of energy and nutrients through different pathways.

questions.explanation: A food web is a graphical representation of interconnected food chains. It shows the feeding relationships and energy flow between different organisms in an ecosystem. A food web starts with primary producers, such as plants, which are then consumed by primary consumers, such as herbivores. The primary consumers are then consumed by secondary consumers, such as carnivores, and so on. Therefore, the statement "Start with primary consumers" is not correct for a food web.

questions.text: In nitrogen fixation, nitrogen is converted to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In nitrogen fixation, nitrogen is converted to Nitrate ions and ammonia. Nitrogen fixation is the process of converting atmospheric nitrogen gas (N2) into usable forms of nitrogen, such as nitrate ions (NO3-) and ammonia (NH3), by certain bacteria, such as Rhizobium, and lightning.

Atomic nitrogen refers to nitrogen in its elemental form, represented as N. It consists of individual nitrogen atoms that are not chemically combined with other elements. Atomic nitrogen is not a product of nitrogen fixation, as the process involves the conversion of atmospheric nitrogen into more complex compounds like nitrate ions and ammonia.

Urea is a compound composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen, with the chemical formula (NH2)2CO. While urea does contain nitrogen, it is not a direct product of nitrogen fixation. Urea is primarily formed as a waste product in animals, including humans, and is excreted through urine. In some cases, urea can be used as a nitrogen fertilizer, but it is not a result of the conversion of atmospheric nitrogen during nitrogen fixation.

This option suggests that all of the given options (nitrate ions and ammonia, atomic nitrogen, and urea) are products of nitrogen fixation. However, this is not accurate. Nitrate ions and ammonia are the primary products of nitrogen fixation, while atomic nitrogen and urea are not directly formed during this process.

questions.explanation: In nitrogen fixation, nitrogen is converted to Nitrate ions and ammonia. Nitrogen fixation is the process of converting atmospheric nitrogen gas (N2) into usable forms of nitrogen, such as nitrate ions (NO3-) and ammonia (NH3), by certain bacteria, such as Rhizobium, and lightning.

questions.text: Which of the following trophic levels has the largest biomass in ecosystems?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Decomposers, such as bacteria and fungi, break down dead organic matter and waste materials, returning nutrients to the ecosystem. While they play a crucial role in nutrient recycling, their biomass is typically lower compared to other trophic levels since they rely on the energy stored in organic matter rather than producing their own energy.

Primary consumers, also known as herbivores, are organisms that consume producers (plants) as their main source of food. Examples include animals like rabbits, deer, and grasshoppers. While primary consumers can have significant biomass, it is generally lower than that of producers since energy is lost as it moves through the food chain.

Secondary consumers are organisms that feed on primary consumers. They are typically carnivores or omnivores. Examples include predators like lions, wolves, and hawks. The biomass of secondary consumers is even lower than that of primary consumers since energy is further lost in each trophic level.

Producers typically have the largest biomass in ecosystems. Producers, also known as autotrophs, are organisms that can convert energy from sunlight or inorganic compounds into organic matter through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis. They include plants, algae, and some bacteria. Since they can produce their own food, they form the base of the food chain and support the energy needs of other organisms in the ecosystem. As a result, the biomass of producers tends to be higher than that of other trophic levels.

Herbivores are primary consumers that specifically feed on plants. They rely on producers for their energy and biomass. While herbivores can have substantial populations and biomass, it is typically lower than that of producers since energy is lost during energy transfer from one trophic level to the next.

questions.explanation: Option A: Decomposers break down dead organic matter and waste materials, playing a crucial role in nutrient recycling. However, their biomass is typically lower compared to other trophic levels. Option B: Primary consumers are herbivores that consume producers (plants) as their main food source. While they can have significant biomass, it is generally lower than that of producers. Option C: Secondary consumers are carnivores or omnivores that feed on primary consumers. Their biomass is even lower than that of primary consumers. Option D: Producers typically have the largest biomass in ecosystems. Producers, also known as autotrophs, are organisms that can convert energy from sunlight or inorganic compounds into organic matter through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis. They include plants, algae, and some bacteria. Since they can produce their own food, they form the base of the food chain and support the energy needs of other organisms in the ecosystem. As a result, the biomass of producers tends to be higher than that of other trophic levels. Option E: Herbivores specifically feed on plants and are primary consumers. While they can have substantial populations and biomass, it is typically lower than that of producers.

questions.text: An area previously supporting life is made barren, the subsequent recolonization is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A climax community is the final stage of succession, remaining relatively unchanged until destroyed by an event such as fire or human interference.

Pioneer succession: The first organisms appearing in the regions of primary succession are pioneer species (usually lichens or mosses). They are the first species present. They must be strong and hardy. The development of a new community is succession.
Primary succession is an ecological succession that begins in essentially lifeless areas, such as regions in which there is no soil or where the soil is incapable of sustaining life.
Secondary succession occurs when the primary ecosystem gets destroyed. For example, a climax community gets destroyed by fire. It gets recolonized after the destruction.

questions.explanation: Secondary succession occurs when the primary ecosystem gets destroyed. For example, a climax community gets destroyed by fire. It gets recolonized after the destruction. A climax community is the final stage of succession, remaining relatively unchanged until destroyed by an event such as fire or human interference. Primary succession is an ecological succession that begins in essentially lifeless areas, such as regions in which there is no soil or where the soil is incapable of sustaining life The pioneer species are hardy species that are the first to colonize barren environments or previously biodiverse steady-state ecosystems that have been disrupted, such as by fire. Therefore, the correct answer is secondary succession.

questions.text: The actual or preserved remains of the organisms that lived in the ancient past are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fossils are the preserved remains, or traces of remains, of ancient organisms.
An impression fossil is formed when the plant or animal decays completely but leaves behind an impression of itself.
This term doesn't fit the given statement.

The term refers to any printed image, irrespective of the printing technique employed, that was created over a period of more than 600 years, from the beginning of printmaking in Europe to the end of the 18th century or early 19th century.

questions.explanation: Fossils are the preserved remains, or traces of remains, of ancient organisms. An impression fossil is formed when the plant or animal decays completely but leaves behind an impression of itself.
